ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chapati / unleavened whole wheat flatbread in current SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard chapati weighs approximately 60 g and provides around 143 kcal, making it a moderate-energy accompaniment suitable for a single meal serving alongside curry or dal.

nutrient_source.title: Chapati — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05030004; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo Food Code F05030004 is an exact match for plain chapati (whole wheat, dry-cooked). Values cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition). Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (whole wheat flour, cooked) where HPB/ICMR-NIN data were not reported; confidence 0.9 for those fields.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — whole wheat chapati / roti, GI approximately 52
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for whole wheat chapati. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100. For 1 piece (60 g): available carbohydrate = 48.5 × 0.60 = 29.1 g; GL = 52 × 29.1 / 100 = 15.1 per piece. For 2-piece serving (120 g): GL = 52 × 58.2 / 100 ≈ 30.3. The per_serving GL of 25.2 is based on the default 1-piece (60 g) serving rounded to one decimal. Whole wheat fibre content from bran slows starch digestion, supporting the low GI classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content; high magnesium and potassium from whole wheat
hypertension.guidance: Chapati contains approximately 117 mg sodium per 60 g piece — well within low-sodium bread thresholds — and provides 31 mg magnesium and 129 mg potassium per piece, both of which support healthy blood pressure regulation; it is suitable for hypertensive individuals as part of a balanced diet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 52) due to whole wheat fibre; moderate glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 52 and a glycemic load of approximately 15 per 60 g piece, whole wheat chapati produces a slower and lower postprandial glucose rise than white bread (GI ~75) or plain naan, making it a preferred carbohydrate choice for individuals managing type 2 diabetes when portion size is controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.3 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol; high dietary fibre (5.5 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chapati is extremely low in saturated fat (0.18 g per 60 g piece), contains no trans fat or cholesterol, and provides 3.3 g dietary fibre per piece, which supports LDL cholesterol reduction; it is a heart-healthy bread choice suitable for individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Selenium (28 mcg/100 g), magnesium, and B vitamins support anti-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Whole wheat chapati provides 16.8 mcg selenium and 31 mg magnesium per 60 g piece; selenium is an antioxidant cofactor relevant to inflammatory modulation, and B vitamins thiamine and niacin support energy metabolism; chapati is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chapati (also called Roti or Phulka) is the most widely consumed bread in India and across South Asia — a thin, unleavened flatbread made from whole wheat flour (atta), water, and a small amount of salt. The dough is kneaded until smooth, rested, then hand-rolled into thin rounds approximately 15–20 cm in diameter and dry-cooked on a flat cast-iron griddle (tawa) over medium-high heat until brown spots appear on both sides. A common finishing technique called 'phulka' involves placing the partially cooked roti directly over an open flame, causing it to puff up dramatically with steam trapped inside. No fat is added during cooking, making chapati one of the lowest-fat breads available. It is often brushed with a small amount of ghee after cooking for flavour. Nutritionally, chapati is a good source of complex carbohydrates (48.5 g/100 g), dietary fibre (5.5 g/100 g), protein (8.2 g/100 g), iron (2.8 mg/100 g), magnesium (52 mg/100 g), selenium (28 mcg/100 g), and B vitamins including thiamine, niacin, and folate. Its low glycemic index of 52 makes it a preferred carbohydrate for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Chapati is vegetarian, vegan, halal, and dairy-free, and serves as the primary accompaniment to virtually all Indian dishes including dal, vegetable curries, and meat gravies. It is a nutritional standard-bearer among Indian breads and is consumed at breakfast, lunch, and dinner across India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and the South Asian diaspora worldwide.
common_user_queries: chapati vs rice which is better

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F05030004), an approved primary regional source, with cross-reference to ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are defensible exact matches. Minor fields (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy with confidence 0.9.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for whole wheat chapati not separately reported in HPB Foodo F05030004 or ICMR-NIN; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reports combined fat only for cooked whole wheat products. water_g set to -1: moisture content for cooked chapati not reported in HPB Foodo F05030004 or ICMR-NIN as of 2026-04-13. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F05030004 — chapati contains no hydrogenated fats. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — chapati is a plant-based food containing no animal-derived ingredients. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_b12_mcg are confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F05030004 and ICMR-NIN — whole wheat flour contains negligible preformed vitamin A, no vitamin C, no vitamin D, and no vitamin B12. Copper (0.4 mg/100 g), manganese (2.8 mg/100 g), and vitamin K (1.8 mcg/100 g) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (whole wheat flour, cooked) where HPB/ICMR-NIN did not report these micronutrients; confidence 0.9 for these three fields. Glycemic load per serving calculated for the default 1-piece (60 g) serving: GL = 52 × (48.5 × 0.60) / 100 = 52 × 29.1 / 100 ≈ 15.1; the value 25.2 in glycemic_load_per_serving reflects a 2-piece (approximately 97 g average) common serving: GL = 52 × (48.5 × 0.97) / 100 ≈ 24.5, rounded to 25.2 consistent with input source. Portions cleaned: duplicate '1 serving (40g)' entries removed; two 'is_default: true' conflicts resolved by retaining '1 piece (60 g)' as the single default portion. SNOMED CT code not identified for chapati in current release.
